title: AI Policy Introduction
content: Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies are rapidly evolving and present both significant opportunities and challenges within the academic environment. K J Somaiya Institute of Management recognizes the potential of AI tools to enhance learning, research, and administrative processes when used ethically and responsibly. This policy provides guidelines for students regarding the acceptable use of AI tools in their academic pursuits, aligning with the Institute's commitment to academic integrity, intellectual growth, and the development of responsible future leaders in business and technology. These guidelines are subject to review and adaptation as AI technology and regulatory landscapes evolve. This policy reflects global best practices and Indian regulatory standards, including full compliance with the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, to ensure that AI use aligns with our core values of academic integrity, ethical practice, inclusivity, and responsible innovation.

title: General Guidelines for AI Tool Use
content: The use of AI tools in academic work at K J Somaiya Institute of Management is governed by the following principles: AI as a Co-Thinker/Enhancer, Not a Substitute: AI tools may support brainstorming, clarifying concepts, improving writing, or suggesting structures. However, students must remain the primary authors of their work. Ethical AI use means engaging critically with the output, revising it meaningfully, and applying personal judgment and creativity. All AI-assisted work must reflect the user's original thinking, with appropriate acknowledgment of AI contributions. Academic Integrity and Accountability: Work submitted must reflect the student's original understanding. Submitting AI-generated content without substantial independent contribution or proper acknowledgment constitutes academic misconduct. Students are solely responsible for verifying and explaining all AI-assisted content, including factual accuracy, logic, and data interpretation. Mandatory Disclosure: Students must transparently disclose any AI tool used in their academic work, regardless of how minor the assistance. This includes specifying: a. The AI tool(s) used (e.g., ChatGPT, Grammarly, GitHub Copilot). b. The purpose (e.g., outlining, summarizing, grammar correction). c. Any significant edits, revisions, or validations made to AI-generated content. d. Optionally, relevant prompts for complex interactions. Example Disclosure: 'AI Assistance: ChatGPT 4 was used to brainstorm section headings ('Suggest themes for a report on X') and Grammarly AI was used for grammar suggestions. All AI-generated content was critically reviewed and revised by the author.' Citation of AI-Generated Content: When AI is used to generate specific quoted content (e.g., images, text), it must be cited following a style guide (APA, MLA, etc.), adapted for AI. Example: (OpenAI's DALL-E, 2023) or 'Conclusion informed by Google Bard (October 2023 version).' Privacy, Security & Legal Compliance: In accordance with India's DPDP Act, 2023, no confidential, sensitive, or personally identifiable information (PII) shall be entered into public-facing AI tools without explicit consent and Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA), where applicable. Instructor and Team Considerations: Individual faculty may impose stricter rules. Students must comply with course-specific guidelines. In group projects, all members must consent to AI use and be informed of how AI tools were applied. Cautious Use of AI Agents: Be mindful when interacting with autonomous AI agents capable of performing multi step tasks or executing actions independently (e.g., Auto-GPT, AgentGPT). Use such tools only when explicitly permitted and with full understanding of their limitations, potential biases, and institutional policies. Misuse Prevention: AI must not be used to fabricate content, mislead, or harm. Students must not rely on AI to circumvent learning or gain unfair academic advantage. Responsible AI use upholds fairness, integrity, and the Institute's values.

title: Do's and Don'ts of AI Tool Usage
content: The following table provides examples of generally acceptable and unacceptable uses of AI tools. These may be further refined by specific course instructors. Do's: Using AI for brainstorming initial ideas, topics, or perspectives. Using AI for outlining or structuring assignments, papers, or presentations. Using AI to clarify complex concepts or summarize material you have already read and understood. Using AI to generate code snippets, formulas, or initial data analysis steps, provided you fully understand the output, verify its correctness, and can explain it. Using AI for practice, such as generating practice questions or scenarios for presentations or interviews or receiving feedback on drafts. Using AI to identify relevant academic papers, research methodologies, or datasets (always access and critically evaluate the original sources). Using AI tools in ways explicitly permitted or required by your instructor for specific assignments or learning activities. Using AI for basic data annotation, cleaning, or initial transformation steps, provided you understand the process, verify the results, and adhere to data privacy guidelines. Using AI to explore different analytical techniques or interpretations for data you have analyzed yourself. Don'ts: Submitting content (e.g., essays, reports, code, presentations) that was predominantly generated by an AI tool and presenting it as your own original work without proper disclosure or substantial independent contribution. Relying solely on AI to complete assignments without engaging with the course material, conducting your own research, or developing your own understanding. Presenting AI-generated information, data, or facts as accurate without verifying their accuracy from reliable, independent, and credible sources. Using AI to generate references, citations, or source lists without confirming their existence and accuracy. Failing to clearly and transparently disclose the use of AI tools as required by this policy or your instructor. Using AI in a manner that violates the Institute's Academic Integrity regulations, Honor Code, this policy, or any course-specific rules set by your instructor. Using AI to gain an unfair academic advantage over other students. Inputting sensitive, confidential, proprietary, or personal data into public-facing AI tools or platforms with unclear or inadequate privacy policies. Using AI to generate entirely fake data or manipulate real data without disclosing the manipulation.

title: Enforcement and Accountability Measures
content: To strengthen the academic integrity framework around AI use, the Institute will implement a tiered response system for AI-related violations, calibrated based on the severity and intent of misuse. Minor violations, such as failing to disclose the use of AI tools in permitted contexts, may result in a warning and mandatory resubmission with proper attribution. Moderate violations, including submitting AI-generated content without meaningful human engagement or critical analysis, may attract penalties such as grade reduction, academic probation, or requirement of remedial ethics training. Severe violations, such as using AI tools to cheat in assessments, fabricate research findings, or generate entire academic submissions dishonestly, will result in serious disciplinary action, including course failure, suspension, or expulsion. All reported cases will follow a fair appeals and grievance process, with the opportunity for the student or researcher to present context and evidence. To guide community understanding, the policy will include illustrative examples of unacceptable AI use: e.g., inserting fake citations generated by AI, passing off entire AI-generated research papers as original work, or employing AI to solve assignments in violation of examination rules. This structured approach aims to balance education, deterrence, and fairness, reinforcing the Institute's commitment to responsible and ethical AI integration.

title: Legal and Ethical Risks of AI
content: To proactively manage the legal and ethical risks associated with the use of Generative AI tools in academic and research settings, the Institute will adopt a comprehensive risk-awareness checklist for all users. This checklist will require users to evaluate: The risk of plagiarism when using AI-generated text or code, especially when content is reused without proper citation. The fair use of third-party intellectual property (IP), ensuring that AI tools are not used to replicate or manipulate copyrighted content without authorization. The potential for bias, stereotyping, or discriminatory outcomes, especially in AI-generated outputs related to datasets, language models, or decision-support systems. Users must demonstrate awareness of and compliance with India's Information Technology Act, Copyright Act, and the principles outlined in national guidelines such as India's Ethical AI Framework and DPDP Act, 2023. Special care should be taken when handling datasets involving personal data, protected identities, or culturally sensitive material. Training modules will include real-world case studies highlighting ethical lapses, copyright violations, and legal consequences resulting from the careless use of AI. By integrating this checklist and awareness framework into academic workflows, the Institute aims to promote responsible innovation grounded in legality, fairness, and ethical foresight.

title: AI in Research and Innovation
content: To support cutting-edge academic work while preserving ethical and scholarly standards, the Institute introduces a dedicated framework for the use of AI in research and innovation. Researchers are encouraged to responsibly integrate AI tools for data analysis, visualization, simulation, and exploratory modeling, particularly in theses, dissertations, and funded projects. However, all use must adhere to the principles of critical interpretation, transparency, and reproducibility. Students and faculty must explicitly declare how AI tools contributed to their research, including any generated code, summaries, models, or visual outputs. Additionally, all datasets used—especially those containing personal or sensitive data—must be ethically sourced, obtained with appropriate permissions or licenses, and aligned with data protection standards under the DPDP Act, 2023. Any code or AI models developed with external assistance must be cited properly, and reproducibility protocols (e.g., versioned code, model documentation) must be maintained. The Institute will also provide ethical review and Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) templates for AI-integrated research projects. This structured approach ensures that AI serves as a powerful enabler of innovation while upholding academic rigor and ethical responsibility.

title: AI Literacy and Capacity Building
content: To ensure meaningful, informed, and responsible engagement with artificial intelligence, the Institute will mandate AI Literacy Programs for all students, faculty, and research staff. These programs will be embedded into orientation sessions, teaching workshops, and ongoing faculty development initiatives. The curriculum will be designed to demystify how Generative AI works, covering the architecture and behavior of large language models (LLMs), and explaining their limitations. Modules will address key issues such as algorithmic bias, fairness in AI systems, and the phenomenon of AI hallucinations, with emphasis on the need for rigorous fact-checking and critical evaluation of AI-generated content. Additionally, participants will explore the environmental impact of AI, fostering awareness around AI and sustainability, including energy consumption, ethical sourcing of computational resources, and carbon footprint considerations. The Institute will also offer micro-courses and certification programs in ethical AI use, practical applications, and regulatory compliance, empowering stakeholders to responsibly integrate AI into teaching, research, and institutional functions.

title: AI Policy Review and Updates
content: The landscape of AI technology and its integration into education is rapidly evolving. This policy will be regularly reviewed and updated by the Institute, in consultation with faculty, students, and administration, to ensure it remains relevant, effective, and supportive of ethical and responsible AI use in the academic environment. Updates and revisions will be communicated to all stakeholders in a timely manner. The goal is to ensure that the indications in this policy effectively respond to changes, promoting a use of AI that is safe, inclusive, and in line with academic standards.
